content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
February 22, 2017 
Zachary D. Wechsler 
Attorney at Law 
21515 Hawthorne Blvd., Suite 610 
Torrance, CA 91503 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-17-038 
Dear Mr. Wechsler: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Mr. Phillip Ihde regarding the 
revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Commission advice is the 
application of the law (of the Act) to a specific set of facts provided by the requestor. We cannot 
provide formal advice regarding hypothetical questions. However, we can provide the following 
informal guidance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided 
by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the provisions of the Act and not under 
other laws such as the Public Contracts Code or Section I 090. 
FACTS 
Mr. Ihde retired from the Bureau of Real Estate (the "Bureau") in December 2016 after a 
little more than 26 years of state service. His final position (which he held for 11 years) was 
Managing Deputy Commissioner IV. As a Managing Deputy Commissioner IV, Mr. Ihde was 
required to fill out a Form 700 each year. While employed by the Bureau, Mr. Ihde did the 
following: 
• Managed, directed and coordinated the investigative operations of enforcement staff for Los 
Angeles, Ventura and Santa Barbara Counties, and the San Diego District Office. 
• Provided functional authority over Los Angeles subdivision personnel, including budget 
approval staff, in cooperation with the Assistant Commissioner, Subdivisions. 
• Made recommendations on settlement proposals to the Assistant Commissioner of 
Enforcement. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. 1-17-038 
Page No. 2 
• Prepared transmittals with recommendations for stipulation and agreements and stipulation 
and waivers for cases handled within Los Angeles Region North. 
• Provided testimony as an investigator in administrative law proceedings against licensed 
individuals and in criminal proceedings brought by various District Attorneys throughout 
the State of California, including testimony in preliminary hearings and jury trials. He has 
also provided sworn declarations for the California Attorney General in matters involving 
both civil and criminal investigations. 
For example, during the course of his employment with the Bureau, Mr. Ihde performed 
Expert Witness services for the District Attorneys Office of Monterey County. At that time, he was 
paid his regular salary by the Bureau and his Expert Fees were paid by the DA 's office to the 
Bureau. Among the subjects Mr. Ihde testified to at that trial were: California laws and regulations 
pertaining to real estate brokers, salespersons, and loan processors, including, but not limited to, 
laws relating to trust fund handling, trust fund accounts, document maintenance, and preservation, 
disclosures, supervision of licenses and unlicensed personnel by designated corporate brokers, real 
estate mortgage loan transactions, and prohibited activities; legally compliant marketing and 
business practices of real estate brokerage companies working pursuant to licenses issued by the 
Bureau; the practices and procedures of the Bureau with respect to investigations, audits, and/or 
licensing revocations; and the duties, obligations, and responsibilities of various persons, licensed 
and unlicensed, involved in real estate transactions. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. In addition, Section 
87407 and 87100 prohibits officials from making, participating in making, or using their position to 
influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment, or have any 
arrangement concerning employment.2 These provisions are commonly referred to as the "revolving 
door" prohibitions. 
One-Year Ban 
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her 
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; Regulation 
18746.1.) 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. 
(Section 87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746.l(a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date 
the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban 
applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or 
2 We do not further discuss Sections 87100 and 87407 since Mr. Ihde has already left employment. 


File No. 1-17-038 
Page No. 3 
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or 
representative of that person. (Regulation 18746. I(b)(3) and (4).) 
In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to certain matters involving specific 
parties such as "judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance 
or communication made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or 
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation 
of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 
18746. l(b)(S).) An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made 
for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or 
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication 
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by 
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Ibid.) 
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (1) before a state 
agency that the public official worked for or represented, (2) before a state agency "which budget, 
personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public official 
worked for or represented, or (3) before any state agency subject to the direction and control of the 
Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office during the twelve 
months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation 18746.1 (b )( 6).) 
Permanent Ban 
The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in certain proceeding involving the State of California and other 
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state 
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 
18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of 
absence from any particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741.1 ( a)( 1 ). ) 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any 
oral or written communication - or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication -made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding 
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. '" Judicial, quasi­
judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other 
determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other 
particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative 
agency . . .. " (Section 87 400( c ). ) 
Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she 
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, 
formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of 
confidential information .... "3 (Section 87400(d).) 
3 Note that Regulation 18741.l(a)(4) provides that "A supervisor is deemed to have panicipated in any 
proceeding that was 'pending before,' as defined in subdivision (b) of Regulation 18438.2, the official's agency and that 


QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS 
File No. 1-17-038 
Page No. 4 
1. Mr. Ihde would like to give advice on how licensees may have broken the law or how 
someone might become and/or remain compliant with various Bureau regulations. 
011e-year ban: As noted above, Section 87406 prohibits certain formal or informal 
appearances, or making any oral or written communications, before the former state employee's 
former agency. This prohibition is not implicated in this question since he would apparently not 
appear before the Bureau. 
Permanent ban: The Permanent Ban prohibits former employees from participating, for 
compensation, in certain proceedings involving the State of California and other specific parties, or 
assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee participated 
while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) For example, if 
Mr. Ihde participated in an enforcement action against an individual and then was hired to advise 
the same individual with respect to fighting this charge, this would be prohibited by Section 87400 
et seq. lfhe did not participate in the proceeding as a former state employee the permanent ban 
would not apply. 
2. Offer opinions to individuals currently under investigation by the Bureau, assuming the 
investigation began after the date of his retirement? 
One-year ban: This prohibition does not apply to merely advising private citizens. 
Permanent ba11: If the investigation begins after his retirement from the state (meaning that 
he has not participated in any aspect of the case while he served as a state administrative official) he 
would not be prohibited from offering opinions to individuals currently under investigation by the 
Bureau. 
3. Testify as an expert witness on behalf of such individuals at an administrative hearing, 
either by way of a declaration or in person either at all, or within one year of his retirement? 
One-year ban: A prohibited appearance before a state administrative agency does not 
include an appearance in a court oflaw or before an administrative law judge. (Section 87406(d), 
see also.) 
was under his or her supervisory authority. For purposes of this regulation, a proceeding is under a supervisor's 
'supervisory authority' if any of the following applies to the supervisor: 
"(A) The supervisor's duties include the primary responsibility within the agency for directing the operation or 
function of the program where the proceeding is initiated or conducted. However, this provision does not apply to a 
supervisor who is only responsible for the general oversight of the administrative actions or functions of a program i 
n which the responsibilities concerning the specific or final review of the proceeding are expressly delegated to other 
persons in the agency. 
"(B) The supervisor directly supervises the person performing the investigation, review, or other action 
involved in the proceeding including, but not limited to, assigning the matter for which the required conduct is taken. 
"(C) The supervisor reviews, discusses, or authorizes any action in the proceeding. 
"(D) The supervisor has contact with any of the participants in the proceeding regarding the subject of the 
proceeding." 


File No. 1-17-038 
Page No. 5 
Perma11e11t ba11: If the pennanent ban applied, Mr. Ihde would be prohibited from appearing 
before his fonner employer. However, Section 87403 contains narrow exemptions from the 
pennanent prohibitions. These exemptions generally allow fonner state officials to provide 
uncompensated expert witness testimony or, in specific circumstances, and with the pennission of 
the agency or a court, to participate more actively in a proceeding that was pending during the 
official's tenure with the agency. Without specific facts, we can only direct you to these 
exemptions. 
4. Ofer guidance to firms and/or individuals who wish to remain compliant with Bureau 
regulations? 
• 
Would the answer be the same if those firms or individuals had business with Mr. Ihde while 
he was employed by the Bureau? 
• 
Would the answer be the same if those firms or individuals had business with the Bureau, 
but not Mr. Ihde during his period of employment by the Bureau; Assume the business he 
had with the firms or individuals was new business? 
One-year ba11: We assume that Mr. Ihde will not be appearing or communicating with the 
Bureau. If so, the one-year ban would not be implicated. As noted above, Section 87406 prohibits 
certain fonnal or infonnal appearances, or making any oral or written communications, before his 
or her fonner agency. This prohibition is not implicated in this question. 
Perma11e11t ban: These facts are vague and as a result we cannot provide guidance except to 
restate the statutory rule that Mr. Ihde would be prohibited from advising clients regarding any 
judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which Mr. Ihde participated. Thus, if "having 
business with" Mr. Ihde means that the client was subject to a judicial, quasi-judicial or other 
proceeding before the Bureau and Mr. [hde participated in the decision as a state employee, he 
would be prohibited from advising. You may wish to consult prior advice letters to find examples 
where Section 87400 et seq. applied and contact us when you have actual facts regarding client 
representation. 
5. May Mr. Ihde offer his expert opinion to District Attorneys and/or defendants in matters 
pertaining to areas of his expertise? 
This seems to be the same question as number 3. 
6. May Mr. Ihde communicate directly with the Bureau, after a one-year period elapses, to 
lobby on behalf of his clients? 
One-year ban: As noted above, Section 87406 prohibits certain fonnal or infonnal 
appearances, or making any oral or written communications, before his or her fonner agency for 
one-year. After one year, Section 87406 is not implicated. 
Per111a11ent ban: Application of the pennanent ban is limited to judicial, quasi-judicial or 
other proceedings before the Bureau in which Mr. Ihde participated. Lobbying regarding a 


File No. 1-17-038 
Page No. 6 
proceeding in which the official did not participate while in state service, for instance, would not 
trigger the permanent ban. 
7. May Mr. Ihde communicate directly with the Bureau to discuss its regulations and offer 
opinions as to how those regulations ought to be enforced in a particular matter? 
We assume this would be after one year. See the prior answer. 
8. Is there any activity Mr. Ihde might pe1form that would be subject to a permanent ban? 
Yes. See the discussion above. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
eneral Counsel 
. Wallace 
t General Counsel 
ivision

grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; Regulation 
18746.1.)

the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban 
applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or

representative of that person. (Regulation 18746. I(b)(3) and (4).)

of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 
18746. l(b)(S).) An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made 
for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or

proceeding before the Bureau and Mr. [hde participated in the decision as a state employee, he 
would be prohibited from advising. You may wish to consult prior advice letters to find examples 
where Section 87400 et seq. applied and contact us when you have actual facts regarding client 
representation. 
5. May Mr. Ihde offer his expert opinion to District Attorneys and/or defendants in matters 
pertaining to areas of his expertise? 
This seems to be the same question as number 3. 
6. May Mr. Ihde communicate directly with the Bureau, after a one-year period elapses, to 
lobby on behalf of his clients? 
One-year ban: As noted above, Section 87406 prohibits certain fonnal or infonnal 
appearances, or making any oral or written communications, before his or her fonner agency for 
one-year. After one year, Section 87406 is not implicated. 
Per111a11ent ban: Application of the pennanent ban is limited to judicial, quasi-judicial or 
other proceedings before the Bureau in which Mr. Ihde participated. Lobbying regarding a  
proceeding in which the official did not participate while in state service, for instance, would not 
trigger the permanent ban. 
7. May Mr. Ihde communicate directly with the Bureau to discuss its regulations and offer 
opinions as to how those regulations ought to be enforced in a particular matter? 
We assume this would be after one year. See the prior answer. 
8. Is there any activity Mr. Ihde might pe1form that would be subject to a permanent ban? 
Yes. See the discussion above.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION · 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 February 22, 2017 Zachary D. Wechsler Attorney at Law 21515 Hawthorne Blvd., Suite 610 Torrance, CA 91503 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1-17-038 Dear Mr. Wechsler: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Mr. Phillip Ihde regarding the revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Commission advice is the application of the law (of the Act) to a specific set of facts provided by the requestor. We cannot provide formal advice regarding hypothetical questions. However, we can provide the following informal guidance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) Please note that we are only providing advice under the provisions of the Act and not under other laws such as the Public Contracts Code or Section I 090. FACTS Mr. Ihde retired from the Bureau of Real Estate (the "Bureau") in December 2016 after a little more than 26 years of state service. His final position (which he held for 11 years) was Managing Deputy Commissioner IV. As a Managing Deputy Commissioner IV, Mr. Ihde was required to fill out a Form 700 each year. While employed by the Bureau, Mr. Ihde did the following: • Managed, directed and coordinated the investigative operations of enforcement staff for Los Angeles, Ventura and Santa Barbara Counties, and the San Diego District Office. • Provided functional authority over Los Angeles subdivision personnel, including budget approval staff, in cooperation with the Assistant Commissioner, Subdivisions. • Made recommendations on settlement proposals to the Assistant Commissioner of Enforcement. 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. 1-17-038 Page No. 2 • Prepared transmittals with recommendations for stipulation and agreements and stipulation and waivers for cases handled within Los Angeles Region North. • Provided testimony as an investigator in administrative law proceedings against licensed individuals and in criminal proceedings brought by various District Attorneys throughout the State of California, including testimony in preliminary hearings and jury trials. He has also provided sworn declarations for the California Attorney General in matters involving both civil and criminal investigations. For example, during the course of his employment with the Bureau, Mr. Ihde performed Expert Witness services for the District Attorneys Office of Monterey County. At that time, he was paid his regular salary by the Bureau and his Expert Fees were paid by the DA 's office to the Bureau. Among the subjects Mr. Ihde testified to at that
